task: I got an unexpected phone call this morning about a financial matter, and I’m trying to decide what to do before I call anyone back. Could you help me think through the first few steps for checking it out and what details I should write down?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a simple, safe way to handle it.

### First steps
1. **Don’t call back using the number from the message**
2. **Check the real account first**
   - bank app, card statement, official website, or the number on the back of the card
3. **See if anything actually changed**
   - balance, pending charges, notices, alerts
4. **Save the message**
   - screenshot, voicemail, caller ID, or email
5. **If it still seems off, call the official number**
   - ask them to verify the issue

### What to write down
- Date and time of the call
- Phone number shown
- Name they used
- What they said the issue was
- Any account or reference number
- What they asked you to do
- Whether they pushed for urgency
- What you found when you checked the real account

### Warning signs
- Pressure to act right away
- Requests for passwords, codes, or full account details
- Asking you to move money
- Telling you not to check with the bank directly
- The call feels rushed or vague

### Simple rule
If it’s real, the official account will show it. If not, don’t trust the call.

If you want, I can turn this into a **short phone-call checklist** you can keep by the phone.

user_simulator.text: That’s useful, but I’d like it a little more exact. What details matter most if I do call the bank or credit card company — like should I ask for the last 4 digits of the account, the department name, time of call, that sort of thing? And maybe a short checklist I can keep by the phone. I don’t want to miss something small and then have to go back over it again.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — those details are worth getting. Keep it simple and write down the few things that matter most.

### What to ask for / write down
- **Date and time**
- **Name of the person you spoke with**
- **Department or team name**
- **Callback number** they give you
- **Last 4 digits of the account** they’re discussing
- **Case, ticket, or reference number**
- **What the issue is**
- **What they want you to do**
- **Any deadline**
- **What they say to do next**

### Good questions to ask
- “Can you give me the **department name**?”
- “What is the **case or reference number**?”
- “Which **account ending in the last 4 digits** are you looking at?”
- “What should I do **next**, and by when?”
- “Is there a **safe number** I should call back on?”

### What not to give out unless you called the official number yourself
- Full Social Security number
- Full account number
- Passwords
- One-time security codes
- Login details
- Debit card PIN

### Simple phone checklist
- [ ] Check the real account first
- [ ] Write down date and time
- [ ] Save the message or number
- [ ] Call the official number on the card or website
- [ ] Ask for department name
- [ ] Ask for last 4 digits of the account
- [ ] Ask for case/reference number
- [ ] Write down what they want you to do
- [ ] Do not give passwords or codes
- [ ] End the call if it feels rushed or pushy

If you want, I can make this into a **very short one-card version** you could keep next to the phone.

user_simulator.text:
Yes, that’s about what I needed. The last 4 digits, department name, callback number, case or reference number — that’s the part I didn’t want to forget.

A little card by the phone would be handy:

**Phone checklist**
- Don’t call back from the number they gave you
- Check the real account first
- Write down date and time
- Write down name and department
- Ask for case/reference number
- Ask which account they mean, last 4 digits only
- Ask what they want you to do and by when
- Don’t give passwords, codes, or full account info
- Hang up if they push too hard

That’s plain enough. I can use that.
